title: Build persistent Story Library with create, rename, archive, and resume
description:
## Problem
The app currently shows empty placeholders and does not let writers manage real stories. Without persistent story records and quick resume, users cannot build a daily writing habit or trust the app for ongoing projects.

## Target user
Solo fiction writers drafting one or more long-form stories who need a reliable home screen and library to manage works-in-progress.

## Outcome / KPI
- Increase weekly active writers who return to an existing story
- At least 70% of writing sessions start from Quick Resume or Stories list after launch
- Reduce first-session drop-off between story creation and first edit

## Scope
- Add story persistence layer for metadata (title, updatedAt, archive flag)
- Stories page supports create, rename, archive/unarchive, and delete-confirmation guardrail
- Home dashboard shows Recent Stories and Quick Resume based on last edited items
- Clicking any story opens `/story/[id]` for editing
- Empty states include clear primary actions (Create story / Restore archived)

## Non-goals
- Real-time multi-user collaboration
- Publishing/sharing workflows
- AI writing assistance

## Acceptance criteria
- User can create a new story and see it appear in Stories and Home Recent list immediately
- Renaming a story persists across reload and navigation
- Archived stories are hidden from default list and recoverable from an archive filter
- Quick Resume always points to most recently edited non-archived story
- Deleting requires explicit confirmation and removes the story from all lists

## Priority rationale
This is the minimum product surface required to make the app usable beyond a demo. It unlocks all downstream drafting and structure features by making stories first-class persisted objects.

## Handoff
Assign to: Market Research

<plan>

## Goal
Build a persistent Story Library that lets writers create, rename, archive, and resume stories with a resume-first Home dashboard, enabling daily writing habits and multi-project management.

## Scope and files

**New files:**
- `src/types/story.ts` - Story data model (title, id, updatedAt, isArchived)
- `src/lib/stories.ts` - Story CRUD functions with localStorage persistence
- `src/components/StoryCard/StoryCard.tsx` - Story card/row component for lists
- `src/components/StoryCard/StoryCard.module.css` - Story card styles
- `src/components/ConfirmDialog/ConfirmDialog.tsx` - Reusable confirmation modal
- `src/components/ConfirmDialog/ConfirmDialog.module.css` - Dialog styles
- `src/components/StoryActionMenu/StoryActionMenu.tsx` - Inline action menu (rename, archive, delete)
- `src/components/StoryActionMenu/StoryActionMenu.module.css` - Menu styles

**Modified files:**
- `src/app/page.tsx` - Wire up Quick Resume and Recent Stories with real data
- `src/app/page.module.css` - Add styles for Resume button and story rows
- `src/app/stories/page.tsx` - Add New Story button, story list, archive toggle
- `src/app/stories/page.module.css` - Add toolbar button and list grid styles

## Step-by-step plan

### 1. Define Story data model and persistence layer
Create `src/types/story.ts`:
- Export `Story` interface with `id: string`, `title: string`, `updatedAt: number` (timestamp), `isArchived: boolean`, `createdAt: number`
- Keep model minimal (no content field yet, only metadata)

Create `src/lib/stories.ts`:
- `getStories(): Story[]` - Load from localStorage `stories` key, return sorted by `updatedAt` desc
- `getStory(id: string): Story | null` - Get single story by id
- `createStory(title: string): Story` - Generate UUID, set timestamps, persist, return story
- `updateStory(id: string, updates: Partial<Pick<Story, 'title' | 'isArchived'>>): Story | null` - Merge updates, update `updatedAt`, persist
- `deleteStory(id: string): void` - Remove from array, persist
- All functions handle localStorage errors gracefully (try/catch with console.error)
- Use `crypto.randomUUID()` for id generation
- Storage key: `stories`

### 2. Build ConfirmDialog component
Create `src/components/ConfirmDialog/ConfirmDialog.tsx`:
- Props: `isOpen: boolean`, `title: string`, `message: string`, `confirmLabel: string`, `onConfirm: () => void`, `onCancel: () => void`, `destructive?: boolean`
- Render modal overlay with dialog (use semantic `<dialog>` element or ARIA role)
- Focus Cancel button by default (safe default)
- ESC key triggers cancel
- Backdrop click triggers cancel
- Style with grayscale tokens, `--shadow-lg`, `--z-modal`

Create `src/components/ConfirmDialog/ConfirmDialog.module.css`:
- Overlay: fixed, full viewport, dark semi-transparent backdrop
- Dialog: centered, white background, padding, border-radius-md, shadow-lg
- Buttons: inline-flex, spacing-md gap
- Destructive variant uses darker text for confirm button

### 3. Build StoryActionMenu component
Create `src/components/StoryActionMenu/StoryActionMenu.tsx`:
- Props: `storyId: string`, `storyTitle: string`, `isArchived: boolean`, `onUpdate: () => void`
- State: `menuOpen: boolean`, `renameDialogOpen: boolean`, `deleteDialogOpen: boolean`, `newTitle: string`
- Render overflow button (three dots or "···") that toggles menu
- Menu options (conditional):
  - **Rename** (always): Opens inline prompt or dialog with text input pre-filled with current title
  - **Archive/Unarchive** (toggle based on `isArchived`): Calls `updateStory` immediately, closes menu, calls `onUpdate`
  - **Delete** (always): Opens ConfirmDialog with story title in message, on confirm calls `deleteStory`, calls `onUpdate`
- Close menu on option select or outside click
- Keyboard: menu items navigable with arrow keys, Enter to select, ESC to close

Create `src/components/StoryActionMenu/StoryActionMenu.module.css`:
- Menu button: icon button, hover state
- Dropdown: absolute positioned, white bg, shadow-md, border
- Menu items: block links/buttons, hover bg fog-200

### 4. Build StoryCard component
Create `src/components/StoryCard/StoryCard.tsx`:
- Props: `story: Story`, `showActions?: boolean`, `onUpdate?: () => void`, `onClick?: () => void`
- Render card with:
  - Story title (h4 or similar, clickable if `onClick` provided)
  - Last edited timestamp (formatted as "Last edited X hours/days ago" using relative time helper or hardcoded formatter)
  - Action menu (if `showActions` is true and `onUpdate` provided)
- Entire card clickable if `onClick` provided (use keyboard-accessible button wrapper)
- Focus state with 2px outline (--color-fashion-300)

Create `src/components/StoryCard/StoryCard.module.css`:
- Card: border, border-radius-md, padding-md, hover state (shadow-sm elevation)
- Desktop: 2-column grid support via parent
- Mobile: full-width stack
- Typography: title uses --font-size-lg, timestamp uses --font-size-sm with --color-cement-400

### 5. Update Home page for Quick Resume and Recent Stories
Modify `src/app/page.tsx`:
- Import `getStories` from `@/lib/stories`
- Load stories in component (client component with `useState` + `useEffect` or Server Component if no interactivity needed; choose client for future optimistic updates)
- Filter non-archived stories, sort by `updatedAt` desc
- **Quick Resume card:**
  - If stories exist, show most recent (index 0): title, "Last edited X ago", and primary button "Resume writing" that navigates to `/story/[id]`
  - If no stories, show current empty state
- **Recent Stories card:**
  - If stories exist, render list (max 5) of `StoryCard` with `onClick` navigating to `/story/[id]` and `showActions={true}` with `onUpdate` triggering re-fetch
  - If no stories, show current empty state
- Add `router.push` navigation using `next/navigation` `useRouter`

Modify `src/app/page.module.css`:
- Add styles for `.resumeButton` (primary CTA: background --color-black-700, color white, padding, border-radius-md)
- Add styles for `.storyList` (vertical stack with spacing-sm gap)
- Add styles for `.timestamp` (--font-size-sm, --color-cement-400)

### 6. Update Stories page for create/list/archive
Modify `src/app/stories/page.tsx`:
- Convert to client component (`'use client'`)
- Import `getStories`, `createStory` from `@/lib/stories`
- State: `stories: Story[]`, `showArchived: boolean`, `isCreating: boolean`
- `useEffect` to load stories on mount
- Computed: `filteredStories = stories.filter(s => s.isArchived === showArchived)`
- **Toolbar:**
  - Left: `New Story` button → calls `createStory("Untitled Story")`, navigates to `/story/[newId]`, sets `isCreating` loading state
  - Center: keep existing search input (not wired yet, placeholder for future)
  - Right: Archive toggle button (e.g., "Show Archived" / "Hide Archived") toggles `showArchived`
- **Stories list:**
  - If `filteredStories.length > 0`, render grid/list of `StoryCard` with `onClick` and `showActions`
  - If empty and `!showArchived`, show "No stories yet" with "Create your first story to get started"
  - If empty and `showArchived`, show "No archived stories" with "Archive a story to see it here"
- On story update (archive/rename/delete), refetch stories with `getStories()`

Modify `src/app/stories/page.module.css`:
- Add `.newStoryButton` (button styles, left-aligned in toolbar)
- Add `.archiveToggle` (toggle button, right-aligned)
- Add `.storiesGrid` (desktop: 2-column grid with gap, mobile: 1-column)
- Add `.emptyState` refinements if needed (already exists, may need minor tweaks)

### 7. Wire story navigation
In `StoryCard` and Home/Stories pages:
- Use `next/navigation` `useRouter().push('/story/' + story.id)` for navigation
- Ensure `/story/[id]/page.tsx` can handle any valid UUID id (it currently does)

### 8. Add accessibility and keyboard support
- All interactive elements must be keyboard-navigable (buttons, menu items, dialogs)
- Focus management: dialog opens → focus Cancel, dialog closes → return focus to trigger
- ARIA: dialog has `role="dialog"`, `aria-labelledby` for title, `aria-describedby` for message
- Screen reader: action menu announces "Actions for [story title]"
- Test tab order: toolbar → story cards → action menus

### 9. Test and polish
- Manual test: Create story, rename, archive, unarchive, delete (with confirmation), resume
- Test edge cases: empty states, long titles (truncation?), very old timestamps
- Test persistence: reload page, check localStorage in DevTools
- Test responsive: desktop 2-col, tablet/mobile 1-col
- Test keyboard: tab through all controls, activate with Enter/Space

## Test plan

**Unit/integration tests (manual):**
1. **Create story:**
   - Click "New Story" on Stories page
   - Verify navigates to `/story/[id]` with new UUID
   - Go back, verify story appears in Stories list and Home Recent Stories
2. **Rename story:**
   - Click action menu on a story → Rename
   - Enter new title, confirm
   - Verify title updates in both Stories list and Home
   - Reload page, verify persistence
3. **Archive story:**
   - Click action menu → Archive
   - Verify story disappears from default list
   - Toggle "Show Archived", verify story appears
   - Unarchive, verify returns to main list
4. **Delete story:**
   - Click action menu → Delete
   - Verify confirmation dialog appears with story title
   - Cancel, verify nothing deleted
   - Delete again, confirm, verify story removed
   - Reload, verify deletion persisted
5. **Quick Resume:**
   - Create/edit multiple stories
   - Verify Home shows most recently edited story in Quick Resume
   - Click "Resume writing", verify navigates to correct story
6. **Empty states:**
   - Delete all stories
   - Verify Home shows "No stories yet" / "Nothing in progress"
   - Verify Stories shows "No stories yet" with create CTA
   - Toggle archive, verify "No archived stories"
7. **Persistence:**
   - Open DevTools → Application → Local Storage
   - Verify `stories` key exists with JSON array
   - Manually corrupt JSON, reload, verify graceful error handling (empty list)

**Accessibility tests:**
- Tab through entire Home and Stories pages, verify all buttons/menus reachable
- Open action menu, navigate with arrow keys, select with Enter
- Open delete dialog, press ESC, verify closes
- Test with VoiceOver/NVDA: verify announcements for action menu, dialog

**Responsive tests:**
- Desktop (1024px+): 2-column story cards, single-row toolbar
- Tablet (768-1023px): 1-column stories, toolbar may wrap
- Mobile (<768px): 1-column, full-width buttons

**Commands:**
```bash
npm run dev
# Manual testing in browser at http://localhost:3000
# Test all CRUD operations, navigation, persistence, accessibility
```

## Risks and mitigations

**Risk 1: Data loss due to localStorage limits or corruption**
- Mitigation: Wrap all localStorage operations in try/catch, log errors to console
- Future: Add localStorage quota check, warn user if nearing limit
- Mitigation: Keep story metadata small (no content in library layer yet)

**Risk 2: Race conditions with rapid create/update/delete**
- Mitigation: Use synchronous localStorage reads/writes (blocking but simple for v1)
- Future: Introduce optimistic UI with rollback on error
- Mitigation: Disable action buttons during async operations (if we add API later)

**Risk 3: Complex state management for archive filter + CRUD updates**
- Mitigation: Keep state simple: single `stories` array, re-fetch on any mutation
- Mitigation: Use `useEffect` dependencies carefully to avoid infinite loops
- Future: Consider React Query or SWR for caching if performance issues arise

**Risk 4: Accessibility gaps in custom dialog/menu components**
- Mitigation: Follow WAI-ARIA dialog and menu patterns strictly
- Mitigation: Test with keyboard only and screen reader before handoff
- Mitigation: Use semantic HTML (`<dialog>`, `<button>`) where possible

**Risk 5: Long story titles breaking layout**
- Mitigation: Add CSS `text-overflow: ellipsis` and `overflow: hidden` to title elements
- Mitigation: Test with 100+ character titles

## Rollback strategy

**If feature must be reverted:**
1. Revert commits for Home/Stories page changes
2. Remove new components (StoryCard, ConfirmDialog, StoryActionMenu)
3. Remove `src/lib/stories.ts` and `src/types/story.ts`
4. Restore original empty state pages from `main` branch
5. Clear localStorage `stories` key in browser (manual step for users, or add migration)

**Data preservation:**
- If rolling back, localStorage `stories` data will remain intact
- User can export localStorage manually if needed (DevTools)
- Future: Add export/import JSON feature to mitigate data loss

**Partial rollback:**
- Can disable individual features (e.g., archive toggle) by feature-flagging in component
- Can simplify UI by removing action menu and keeping only create/navigate

## Observability

**Logs:**
- Console log on story create: `"Created story: [id]"`
- Console log on story update: `"Updated story [id]: [changes]"`
- Console log on story delete: `"Deleted story: [id]"`
- Console error on localStorage failures: `"Failed to load/save stories: [error]"`

**Metrics (future):**
- Track story creation count (localStorage `storyCreatedCount` counter)
- Track Quick Resume usage (log when Resume button clicked)
- Track archive/delete frequency

**Debugging:**
- Add DevTools localStorage inspector workflow to troubleshooting docs
- Include sample localStorage JSON in component comments for reference
- Ensure all error paths log to console (no silent failures)

**User-visible state:**
- Loading state: skeleton rows or "Loading..." text during initial load (if async)
- Error state: inline banner "Failed to load stories. Please refresh." (if localStorage errors)
- Success feedback: optimistic UI (story appears immediately after create), no toast spam

## Acceptance mapping

| Acceptance Criterion | Implementation |
|---------------------|----------------|
| User can create a new story and see it appear in Stories and Home Recent list immediately | Step 5 (Home) + Step 6 (Stories): `createStory()` + optimistic UI + navigation |
| Renaming a story persists across reload and navigation | Step 3 (StoryActionMenu): `updateStory()` with localStorage persistence |
| Archived stories are hidden from default list and recoverable from an archive filter | Step 6 (Stories page): `showArchived` filter toggle |
| Quick Resume always points to most recently edited non-archived story | Step 5 (Home): sort by `updatedAt` desc, filter `!isArchived`, take first |
| Deleting requires explicit confirmation and removes the story from all lists | Step 2 (ConfirmDialog) + Step 3 (StoryActionMenu): delete confirmation with title in message |

**Additional acceptance:**
- Keyboard users can navigate and perform all CRUD operations → Step 8 (accessibility)
- Empty states show clear primary actions → Step 5 + Step 6 (empty state refinements)
- Responsive layout works on mobile/tablet/desktop → Step 6 (responsive CSS)

## Handoff

Implementation plan complete. All steps are sequenced and map to acceptance criteria. Ready for Code Monkey.

Assign to: Code Monkey


</plan>

title: Add chapter and scene navigator with drag-and-drop ordering
description:
## Problem
Writers need structure while drafting, but the current editor is a single undifferentiated text area. Without chapters/scenes, users cannot plan pacing, reorder narrative beats, or navigate long manuscripts efficiently.

## Target user
Novelists and serial fiction writers managing multi-chapter drafts who need scene-level organization similar to Living Writer clarity, without extra clutter.

## Outcome / KPI
- Increase average number of scenes created per active manuscript
- Improve navigation efficiency (time to jump to target scene)
- Reduce manuscript abandonment after chapter 1

## Scope
- Add left-side manuscript tree: chapters with nested scenes
- Support create/edit/delete for chapters and scenes
- Drag-and-drop reorder for chapters and scenes (with keyboard-accessible fallback controls)
- Editor writes to selected scene, not global document blob
- Persist ordering and selection across reloads

## Non-goals
- Timeline visualization
- Character/worldbuilding ontology
- Collaboration features

## Acceptance criteria
- User can create chapter + scene and switch between scenes without data loss
- Reordering scenes updates persisted order and survives refresh
- Scene title updates reflect in navigator and editor header
- Deleting a scene requires confirmation and cannot silently remove content
- Keyboard users can reorder without mouse-only interactions

## Priority rationale
Structured drafting is a core differentiator versus plain document tools and is foundational for future outlining, continuity, and revision intelligence. Shipping this now moves the app from generic editor to story-first product.

## Handoff
Assign to: Market Research

title: Ship reliable autosave + crash recovery with visible save state
description:
## Problem
Writers lose trust immediately when they fear losing words. The current editing experience has no visible save confidence, no recovery workflow, and no resilience guidance when network/storage failures occur.

## Target user
Daily writers with long sessions who need confidence that every sentence is safe, especially on laptops/mobile where interruptions are common.

## Outcome / KPI
- Reduce reported lost-work incidents to near zero
- Increase average session length and return rate
- Improve user trust score for reliability after first week of use

## Scope
- Autosave current scene content on idle interval and on navigation/unload
- Display explicit save state in editor header (`Saving…`, `Saved`, `Save failed`)
- Keep local recovery snapshot for unsynced edits and offer one-click restore
- Add non-blocking error banner with retry action when save fails
- Record last-saved timestamp for user reassurance

## Non-goals
- Full version history UI
- Multi-device merge conflict resolution
- Real-time collaboration editing

## Acceptance criteria
- Typing triggers autosave without manual action
- Save state updates correctly during save lifecycle
- On simulated crash/reload, user can restore unsaved local changes
- Failed save shows actionable retry path and does not silently discard edits
- Last-saved timestamp is visible and updates after successful save

## Priority rationale
Reliability is table stakes for writer retention and prerequisite for premium features. Shipping visible trust signals and recovery now reduces churn risk before expanding advanced tooling.

## Handoff
Assign to: Market Research

title: Launch first-story onboarding flow with guided chapter and scene scaffold
description:
## Problem
New users face a blank-page experience and often fail to reach a meaningful first writing milestone. Without guided setup, activation and early retention suffer.

## Target user
First-time users evaluating the app in their first session, especially writers migrating from generic docs tools.

## Outcome / KPI
- Increase activation rate: % of new users who create first story and first scene in one session
- Increase day-1 to day-7 retention for users who complete onboarding
- Reduce first-session drop-off before first 200 words

## Scope
- Add an optional onboarding flow after first story creation with 3 guided steps:
  1. Name story + genre intent
  2. Create first chapter and first scene
  3. Set a lightweight first-session objective (draft/revise/brainstorm)
- Pre-fill the editor with a minimal scaffold (chapter title + scene prompt placeholder)
- Provide skip and dismiss controls so experienced users can bypass onboarding
- Track onboarding completion state per user to avoid repeated prompts

## Non-goals
- Full interactive tutorial for every feature
- Mandatory onboarding gates that block editor access
- Personalized AI coaching in onboarding v1

## Acceptance criteria
- New user is offered onboarding once after first story creation
- User can complete onboarding in under 2 minutes and land in editable first scene
- User can skip onboarding at any step and still access core editor
- Onboarding completion/skip state persists and prevents repeated prompting
- Event instrumentation exists for each onboarding step and completion outcome

## Priority rationale
The app already has strong drafting/planning surface area; onboarding is the missing bridge from sign-up intent to actual writing behavior. Shipping this now should improve activation before adding more advanced features.

## Handoff
Assign to: Market Research

title: Add scene context metadata (POV, location, timeframe) with filterable navigator
description:
## Problem
Writers lose narrative orientation when drafting across many scenes because key context (POV, location, timeframe) is not consistently visible or searchable. This increases continuity mistakes and slows revision.

## Target user
Multi-chapter fiction writers who juggle multiple POV characters, locations, and timelines while drafting and revising.

## Outcome / KPI
- Reduce continuity-related scene revisions per manuscript
- Increase weekly active use of scene navigator filters
- Decrease average time to find target scene during revision

## Scope
- Add scene-level metadata fields: `POV character`, `location`, `timeframe` (free text v1 with optional suggestions from existing worldbuilding entities)
- Surface metadata in scene cards/navigator rows and at top of scene editor
- Add filter controls in chapter/scene navigator for POV, location, and timeframe
- Keep metadata editable from both scene settings and scene card quick-edit
- Persist metadata for use in existing timeline/continuity workflows

## Non-goals
- Automatic contradiction detection rules
- Complex ontology management for tags/entities
- AI-generated scene metadata

## Acceptance criteria
- User can set/edit POV, location, and timeframe on any scene
- Scene navigator can filter by each metadata field independently and in combination
- Active filters are clearly visible and can be reset in one action
- Metadata appears in the scene editor header without obstructing writing flow
- Metadata values persist across reload and are available in timeline views

## Priority rationale
This builds a practical writing-flow advantage aligned with Living Writer/Campfire inspiration: keep structure context visible while drafting, not hidden in separate planning screens. It also compounds value of in-flight navigator and timeline features already on the roadmap.

## Handoff
Assign to: Market Research

title: Add corkboard-style scene cards for story planning
description:
## Problem
Linear document editing alone makes it hard to visualize narrative flow and quickly spot pacing or sequencing issues.

## Target user
Plot-driven writers who plan scenes before or during drafting.

## Outcome / KPI
- Increase usage of planning views before drafting sessions.
- Improve completion rate of planned scenes to drafted scenes.

## Scope
- Introduce a corkboard/planning view with one card per scene.
- Card fields: scene title, chapter, short summary, status (planned/drafting/done), and drag reorder position.
- Clicking a card opens that scene in the editor.
- Keep card order synced with chapter/scene structure order.

## Non-goals
- Full kanban workflow customization.
- Reader/community feedback features.
- Mobile-native app redesign.

## Acceptance criteria
- Writer can switch between editor view and corkboard view.
- Scene cards can be reordered and updates are persisted.
- Scene status changes are saved and visible in both corkboard and structure sidebar.
- Opening a card routes to the correct scene draft.

## Priority rationale
A corkboard is a high-value differentiator for structured fiction writing and aligns with the primary inspiration product behavior.

## Handoff
Assign to: Market Research

<plan>

## REVISION (2026-03-10)

### Code Review feedback addressed

Code Reviewer identified critical state management and DnD strategy issues. This revision adds corrective steps to fix them before final approval.

### Revision steps (execute these first, in order)

**Rev-Step 1: Fix Corkboard state synchronization (CRITICAL)**

**File:** `src/components/Corkboard/Corkboard.tsx`

**Problem:** Line 79 creates stale state. When parent passes fresh `scenes` prop (e.g., after localStorage update), the internal `useState` does NOT re-sync. This breaks bidirectional data flow.

**Current code:**
```tsx
const [scenes, setScenes] = useState(initialScenes);
```

**Fix:** Add `useEffect` to sync state when prop changes:
```tsx
import { useState, useEffect } from 'react';

// ... inside Corkboard component:
const [scenes, setScenes] = useState(initialScenes);

useEffect(() => {
  setScenes(initialScenes);
}, [initialScenes]);
```

**Alternative (simpler, preferred):** Remove local state entirely and use prop directly with optimistic update callback. For v1, keep the `useEffect` approach to minimize refactor risk.

**Test:** After reordering scenes in Corkboard, if parent component re-fetches scenes from localStorage, verify the corkboard UI updates correctly (no stale order).

---

**Rev-Step 2: Fix story page scene loading performance (MAJOR)**

**File:** `src/app/story/[id]/page.tsx`

**Problem:** Line 19 calls `getScenes(id)` on every render, causing unnecessary localStorage reads and preventing reactivity to data changes.

**Current code:**
```tsx
const scenes = getScenes(id);
```

**Fix:** Move to `useState` + `useEffect` for controlled loading and reactivity:
```tsx
const [scenes, setScenes] = useState<Scene[]>([]);
const [scenesLoading, setLoading] = useState(true);

useEffect(() => {
  setLoading(true);
  const loadedScenes = getScenes(id);
  setScenes(loadedScenes);
  setLoading(false);
}, [id]);

// Add refresh callback for when Corkboard updates localStorage:
const refreshScenes = () => {
  const refreshedScenes = getScenes(id);
  setScenes(refreshedScenes);
};

const handleSceneReorder = (sceneId: string, newOrder: number) => {
  updateSceneOrder(id, sceneId, newOrder);
  refreshScenes(); // Re-fetch after update
};

const handleSceneStatusChange = (sceneId: string, status: SceneStatus) => {
  updateSceneStatus(id, sceneId, status);
  refreshScenes(); // Re-fetch after update
};
```

**Pass `loading` to Corkboard:**
```tsx
<Corkboard
  scenes={scenes}
  loading={scenesLoading}
  onSceneClick={handleSceneClick}
  onReorder={handleSceneReorder}
  onStatusChange={handleSceneStatusChange}
/>
```

**Test:** Verify `getScenes` is only called when `id` changes or after explicit updates (not on every component render). Check browser DevTools React Profiler for reduced re-renders.

---

**Rev-Step 3: Fix drag-and-drop strategy for grid layout (MAJOR)**

**File:** `src/components/Corkboard/Corkboard.tsx`

**Problem:** Line 158 uses `verticalListSortingStrategy`, which is designed for single-column vertical lists. The implementation uses a **responsive grid** (3-4/2/1 columns), causing poor drag UX and incorrect drop zones.

**Current code:**
```tsx
import { verticalListSortingStrategy } from '@dnd-kit/sortable';
// ...
strategy={verticalListSortingStrategy}
```

**Fix:** Use `rectSortingStrategy` for grid/multi-column layouts:
```tsx
import { rectSortingStrategy } from '@dnd-kit/sortable';
// ...
strategy={rectSortingStrategy}
```

**Test:** In desktop view (3-4 columns), drag a card from left column to right column. Verify drop zones appear correctly and card lands in expected position. Repeat for tablet (2 columns) and mobile (1 column) to ensure strategy adapts.

---

**Rev-Step 4: Clean up redundant defensive code (MINOR, optional)**

**File:** `src/components/SceneCard/SceneCard.tsx`

**Problem:** Lines 22-28 implement tagName check to prevent card click when clicking select, BUT the select already has `stopPropagation()` on both `onChange` (line 18) and `onClick` (line 62). This creates redundant defensive layers.

**Current approach:** Both stopPropagation AND tagName check.

**Recommendation (Code Reviewer):** Choose one approach:
- **Option A (preferred):** Keep `stopPropagation` on select (more React-idiomatic), remove tagName check in `handleCardClick`.
- **Option B:** Keep tagName check, remove one `stopPropagation` call.

**Fix (Option A):**
```tsx
const handleCardClick = (e: React.MouseEvent) => {
  // stopPropagation on select handlers is sufficient, no need for tagName check
  onClick();
};
```

Verify select `stopPropagation` remains on lines 18 and 62.

**Test:** Click select dropdown → verify card does NOT open. Click card body (not select) → verify card opens. Both should work with cleaner code.

**Note:** This is MINOR and non-blocking. If risk-averse, defer to future cleanup ticket.

---

### Validation after revision

After implementing Rev-Steps 1-3 (Rev-Step 4 optional):

1. Run `npm run build` → verify no TypeScript errors
2. Manual test sequence:
   - Open corkboard view
   - Reorder 2-3 scenes via drag-and-drop
   - Verify localStorage is updated (`localStorage.getItem('story-1-scenes')` in DevTools console)
   - Change scene status
   - Toggle to Editor mode and back to Corkboard
   - **Verify scene order and status remain correct (no stale state)**
   - Open another browser tab at same story route
   - Verify corkboard shows correct order (cross-tab localStorage sync)
3. Responsive test: Resize browser to desktop/tablet/mobile breakpoints, verify drag-and-drop works smoothly in all layouts
4. Keyboard test: Use arrow keys to reorder scenes, verify grid strategy supports keyboard navigation

### Commit and handoff

After validation passes:

1. Stage changes: `git add src/components/Corkboard/Corkboard.tsx src/app/story/[id]/page.tsx src/components/SceneCard/SceneCard.tsx`
2. Commit with clear message:
   ```bash
   git commit -m "Fix state sync, performance, and DnD strategy (BIN-64 revisions)

   - Add useEffect to sync Corkboard state with prop changes (critical)
   - Move getScenes to useState+useEffect for controlled loading (major)
   - Switch to rectSortingStrategy for grid layouts (major)
   - Simplify SceneCard click handling (minor, optional)

   Addresses Code Reviewer feedback: stale state bug, performance regression,
   and incorrect drag strategy for multi-column grid.

   Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.5 <noreply@anthropic.com>"
   ```
3. Push to branch: `git push origin ticket/BIN-64`
4. Comment on ticket with summary and handoff to Code Reviewer

---



## Goal

Deliver a corkboard-style scene planning view inside the existing story editor route (`/story/[id]`) that allows writers to visualize scenes as cards, reorder them via drag-and-drop, update scene status, and quickly jump into drafting by clicking a card.

## Scope and files

**New files to create:**
- `src/components/Corkboard/Corkboard.tsx` - Main corkboard container component
- `src/components/Corkboard/Corkboard.module.css` - Corkboard styles
- `src/components/SceneCard/SceneCard.tsx` - Individual scene card component
- `src/components/SceneCard/SceneCard.module.css` - Scene card styles
- `src/components/ViewModeSwitch/ViewModeSwitch.tsx` - Editor/Corkboard toggle
- `src/components/ViewModeSwitch/ViewModeSwitch.module.css` - Toggle styles
- `src/types/scene.ts` - Scene data model type definitions
- `src/lib/scenes.ts` - Scene data management utilities (initially mock data, later backend integration)

**Files to modify:**
- `src/app/story/[id]/page.tsx` - Add view mode state and conditional rendering
- `src/app/story/[id]/page.module.css` - Adjust layout for corkboard mode
- `src/components/AppShell/AppShell.tsx` - Update page title logic if needed for corkboard mode

## Step-by-step plan

### Phase 1: Data model and infrastructure (foundation)

**Step 1.1** - Define scene data types (`src/types/scene.ts`)
- Create `Scene` interface with fields: `id`, `title`, `chapter`, `summary`, `status` (planned/drafting/done), `order` (number for position)
- Create `SceneStatus` type union
- Export types for use across components

**Step 1.2** - Create mock scene data utilities (`src/lib/scenes.ts`)
- Export `getScenes(storyId: string)` function returning mock Scene[] (4-5 sample scenes for development)
- Export `updateSceneOrder(storyId: string, sceneId: string, newOrder: number)` stub (console.log for now)
- Export `updateSceneStatus(storyId: string, sceneId: string, status: SceneStatus)` stub
- Document TODO comment for future backend integration

### Phase 2: Core UI components (building blocks)

**Step 2.1** - Build ViewModeSwitch component
- Create toggle button group with "Editor" and "Corkboard" options
- Props: `mode: 'editor' | 'corkboard'`, `onChange: (mode) => void`
- Styles: compact inline button group, active state uses `--color-black-700` text + `--color-fog-200` background, inactive uses `--color-cement-400`
- Use existing spacing/border tokens
- Ensure keyboard navigation (tab + enter/space to switch)
- Add ARIA labels for screen readers

**Step 2.2** - Build SceneCard component
- Props: `scene: Scene`, `onClick: () => void`, `onStatusChange: (status: SceneStatus) => void`, `isDragging?: boolean`
- Layout order: scene title (h3, `--font-size-lg`, `--font-weight-semibold`), chapter label (small, `--color-cement-400`), summary (2-3 line clamp, `--font-size-sm`), status select/buttons, drag handle icon
- Styles: white card with `--color-fashion-300` border, `--shadow-sm`, padding `--spacing-md`, border-radius `--border-radius-md`
- Hover: `--shadow-md`, subtle border color shift to `--color-elephant-500`
- Focus: 2px outline `--color-carbon-600` per accessibility guideline
- Status control: native `<select>` or button group (prefer select for simplicity, style with tokens)
- Drag handle: use CSS cursor grab/grabbing, simple icon or `⋮⋮` character styled with `--color-cement-400`
- Ensure entire card is clickable (except status control area) to open scene

**Step 2.3** - Build Corkboard component
- Props: `scenes: Scene[]`, `onSceneClick: (sceneId: string) => void`, `onReorder: (sceneId: string, newOrder: number) => void`, `onStatusChange: (sceneId: string, status: SceneStatus) => void`
- Layout: board toolbar (scene count, optional filter placeholder), responsive card grid below
- Grid: CSS Grid with `grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fill, minmax(280px, 1fr))`, gap `--spacing-lg`
- Toolbar: flexbox row, scene count on left (`{scenes.length} scenes`), future filter controls on right (add empty div for now)
- Background: `--color-polar-100`
- Empty state: centered message "No scenes yet. Create your first scene to get started." in `--color-cement-400`
- Loading state: render 6 skeleton cards (gray boxes with `--color-fog-200` background)
- Use SceneCard component for each scene
- Integrate drag-and-drop library (recommend `@dnd-kit/core` and `@dnd-kit/sortable` for accessibility and SSR support)
- On reorder: call `onReorder` callback with new position, update local state optimistically

**Step 2.4** - Add drag-and-drop dependency
- Run `npm install @dnd-kit/core @dnd-kit/sortable @dnd-kit/utilities`
- Document in plan: this library chosen for keyboard accessibility, SSR compatibility with Next.js, and active maintenance
- Update package.json (automatic via npm install)

### Phase 3: Story page integration (wiring)

**Step 3.1** - Update story page for view mode switching
- Import ViewModeSwitch, Corkboard components
- Add `'use client'` directive (state management required)
- Add state: `const [viewMode, setViewMode] = useState<'editor' | 'corkboard'>('editor')`
- Load mock scenes: `const scenes = getScenes(id)` (later replace with API call)
- Add ViewModeSwitch in `editorHeader` div (after title input or in separate header row)
- Conditionally render: if `viewMode === 'editor'` show existing editor+sidebar, if `viewMode === 'corkboard'` show `<Corkboard ... />`
- Wire Corkboard callbacks:
  - `onSceneClick`: navigate to scene (placeholder: `console.log('Navigate to scene:', sceneId)` - document future router.push integration)
  - `onReorder`: call `updateSceneOrder` from lib
  - `onStatusChange`: call `updateSceneStatus` from lib

**Step 3.2** - Adjust page layout CSS for corkboard mode
- In `page.module.css`, add `.corkboardMode` class
- When in corkboard mode, replace `.editorLayout` grid with single column, full height for corkboard
- Consider adding data attribute to page container (`data-view-mode={viewMode}`) for conditional styling
- Ensure sidebar remains hidden in corkboard mode (already handled by conditional render, verify)

### Phase 4: Responsive behavior (mobile/tablet)

**Step 4.1** - Add responsive grid breakpoints to Corkboard
- Desktop (>=1024px): `grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fill, minmax(300px, 1fr))` (3-4 columns)
- Tablet (768-1023px): `grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fill, minmax(350px, 1fr))` (2 columns)
- Mobile (<768px): `grid-template-columns: 1fr` (1 column)
- Use CSS media queries in `Corkboard.module.css`

**Step 4.2** - Ensure ViewModeSwitch is accessible on mobile
- Verify toggle remains visible in story header on small screens
- If title + toggle is cramped, consider stacking vertically or reducing title font size (check Design brief guidance)
- Test tap targets are minimum 44x44px per accessibility standards

### Phase 5: Accessibility and keyboard support

**Step 5.1** - Keyboard navigation audit
- Tab order: title input → view mode toggle → scene cards (when in corkboard) → status controls
- Ensure `@dnd-kit/sortable` keyboard reorder works (up/down arrow to move cards, Space to pick up/drop)
- Add keyboard shortcut hint in toolbar (optional): "Use arrow keys to reorder scenes"
- Test with keyboard-only navigation (no mouse)

**Step 5.2** - ARIA labels and roles
- Corkboard toolbar: `role="toolbar"`, `aria-label="Scene planning controls"`
- Scene card grid: `role="list"`, individual cards `role="listitem"`
- Status select: `aria-label="Scene status for {scene.title}"`
- Drag handle: `aria-label="Drag to reorder"`

**Step 5.3** - Focus management
- When switching from Editor to Corkboard, focus should move to corkboard container (use `useRef` + `focus()`)
- When clicking a card to open scene, ensure editor textarea receives focus (future implementation note)

### Phase 6: Error states and edge cases

**Step 6.1** - Handle empty scenes gracefully
- Show empty state message in Corkboard when `scenes.length === 0`
- Include call-to-action hint (e.g., "Create scenes in the editor to see them here")

**Step 6.2** - Handle reorder failures (future-proof)
- Wrap `onReorder` in try-catch, if error occurs show inline alert banner at top of corkboard
- Alert: red border, `--color-black-700` text, "Failed to save scene order. Please try again." with retry button
- On retry: call `onReorder` again
- Rollback optimistic update if failure persists

**Step 6.3** - Loading and transition states
- Add loading prop to Corkboard, render skeleton cards when true
- Ensure smooth transition between editor/corkboard (no layout shift)

### Phase 7: Testing and polish

**Step 7.1** - Manual testing checklist (see Test Plan section)

**Step 7.2** - Visual polish pass
- Verify all spacing uses token values (no hardcoded pixels)
- Check card shadows, borders, hover states look consistent
- Ensure text truncation for long summaries works (CSS `line-clamp`)
- Check focus outlines are visible and high-contrast

**Step 7.3** - Code review prep
- Remove any console.logs (except documented TODOs for backend integration)
- Add JSDoc comments to exported functions/components
- Ensure all components have proper TypeScript types (no `any`)

## Test plan

### Unit/Integration tests (manual for v1, document for future automation)

1. **Scene data model**
   - Verify `getScenes` returns mock data with correct shape
   - Verify `updateSceneOrder` and `updateSceneStatus` log correctly

2. **ViewModeSwitch component**
   - Click "Editor" → verify `onChange` called with 'editor'
   - Click "Corkboard" → verify `onChange` called with 'corkboard'
   - Tab to toggle, press Enter → verify mode switches
   - Verify active state styling shows correctly

3. **SceneCard component**
   - Click card (not status control) → verify `onClick` called
   - Change status dropdown → verify `onStatusChange` called with new status
   - Verify title, chapter, summary render correctly
   - Verify 2-3 line clamp on long summaries
   - Hover card → verify shadow increases
   - Tab to card, press Enter → verify click handler fires

4. **Corkboard component**
   - Render with empty scenes array → verify empty state message shows
   - Render with 5 scenes → verify 5 cards render
   - Drag scene card → verify order changes in UI
   - Drop scene card → verify `onReorder` callback fires with correct new order
   - Change scene status in card → verify `onStatusChange` fires
   - Click scene card → verify `onSceneClick` fires with scene ID

5. **Story page integration**
   - Default view is Editor mode
   - Click Corkboard toggle → view switches, corkboard shows with mock scenes
   - Click Editor toggle → view switches back to editor
   - In Corkboard, reorder scenes → verify console log shows updated order
   - In Corkboard, click scene card → verify console log shows scene ID

### Responsive tests

- Desktop (1280px wide): verify 3-4 columns of cards, sidebar visible in editor mode
- Tablet (768px wide): verify 2 columns of cards, sidebar hidden
- Mobile (375px wide): verify 1 column, view toggle remains accessible

### Accessibility tests

- Keyboard-only navigation: verify can switch modes, reorder scenes, change status without mouse
- Screen reader: verify ARIA labels read correctly (use VoiceOver on Mac or NVDA/JAWS on Windows)
- Focus visibility: verify 2px outlines appear on all interactive elements
- Color contrast: verify text meets WCAG AA standards (4.5:1 for normal text, 3:1 for large text)

### Cross-browser tests

- Chrome (latest): primary test browser
- Safari (latest): verify iOS/Mac compatibility
- Firefox (latest): verify drag-and-drop works
- Edge (latest): quick smoke test

### Commands to run

```bash
# Development server
npm run dev

# Build for production (ensure no TypeScript errors)
npm run build

# Lighthouse audit (accessibility score should be 90+)
# Run in Chrome DevTools on /story/1 in both editor and corkboard modes
```

## Risks and mitigations

**Risk 1: Drag-and-drop library incompatible with Next.js SSR**
- Mitigation: Use `@dnd-kit` which explicitly supports SSR and is well-documented with Next.js
- Fallback: If SSR issues arise, lazy-load Corkboard with `dynamic(() => import(...), { ssr: false })`

**Risk 2: No backend/database yet, scene data not persisted**
- Mitigation: Use localStorage as interim persistence layer (add in Step 1.2)
- Document clear TODO comments for backend API integration
- Plan for API routes in future ticket (e.g., `GET/POST /api/stories/:id/scenes`)

**Risk 3: Overlap/confusion with BIN-61 (outline corkboard)**
- Mitigation: This ticket focuses on scene-level cards; coordinate with Product/Design on BIN-61 scope to avoid duplication
- If BIN-61 is for chapter/outline level, this ticket remains scene-focused and distinct

**Risk 4: Accessibility with drag-and-drop on mobile/touch**
- Mitigation: `@dnd-kit` includes touch support and keyboard fallbacks
- Test on actual mobile devices (iOS Safari, Android Chrome) not just browser emulation
- Provide alternate reorder UI if drag fails (up/down buttons as fallback)

**Risk 5: Performance with large scene lists (50+ scenes)**
- Mitigation: Initially load all scenes (acceptable for <100), add virtualization later if needed
- Use React key prop correctly to avoid re-render thrashing during drag
- Profile with React DevTools if jank is observed

## Rollback strategy

Since this feature is additive (new view mode, not replacing existing editor):

1. **Feature flag approach** (recommended for production):
   - Add environment variable `NEXT_PUBLIC_ENABLE_CORKBOARD=true` in `.env.local`
   - Wrap ViewModeSwitch render in conditional: `{process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_ENABLE_CORKBOARD === 'true' && <ViewModeSwitch ... />}`
   - If issues arise in production, set to `false` and redeploy (instant rollback)

2. **Git rollback**:
   - Changes are localized to `/story/[id]` route and new components
   - Can revert commit or branch merge if critical issues found
   - Existing editor functionality unaffected by corkboard code

3. **Graceful degradation**:
   - If drag-and-drop fails, cards still render (just no reorder)
   - If scene data fails to load, show empty state
   - View mode defaults to "Editor" so users see familiar interface

## Observability

**Logging:**
- Add console.log in `updateSceneOrder` and `updateSceneStatus` stubs for development debugging
- When backend integrated, ensure API errors are logged with scene ID and operation type

**Metrics (future):**
- Track view mode switches (how often users toggle to Corkboard)
- Track scene reorders (frequency, success rate)
- Track scene status changes (planned → drafting → done flow)
- Measure time-to-first-draft after corkboard planning session

**Error tracking:**
- Wrap data operations in try-catch blocks
- Log errors to console in dev, send to error tracking service (e.g., Sentry) in production
- Monitor for: drag-and-drop failures, data load failures, persistence failures

**User feedback:**
- Add optional feedback link in corkboard toolbar (future iteration)
- Track feature usage in analytics (e.g., "corkboard_viewed", "scene_reordered")

## Acceptance mapping

Mapping to ticket acceptance criteria:

**AC1: "Writer can switch between editor view and corkboard view"**
- Steps 2.1, 3.1: ViewModeSwitch component with state management
- Test plan: verify toggle functionality in both directions

**AC2: "Scene cards can be reordered and updates are persisted"**
- Steps 1.2, 2.3, 3.1: Drag-and-drop with `@dnd-kit`, `onReorder` callback
- Steps 1.2 (localStorage interim persistence), 6.2 (error handling)
- Test plan: verify drag reorder triggers `updateSceneOrder` call

**AC3: "Scene status changes are saved and visible in both corkboard and structure sidebar"**
- Steps 2.2, 2.3: Status control in SceneCard, `onStatusChange` callback
- NOTE: Sidebar structure sync requires future ticket (sidebar currently shows placeholder)
- For v1: status visible in corkboard cards, persisted in scene data
- Document TODO: sync sidebar with scene status when sidebar is implemented

**AC4: "Opening a card routes to the correct scene draft"**
- Step 3.1: `onSceneClick` callback (console.log for v1)
- Document TODO: integrate with Next.js router to navigate to scene-specific editor state
- Test plan: verify click handler fires with correct scene ID

**Design brief alignment:**
- Grayscale tokens only: Steps 2.1, 2.2, 2.3 use `--color-*` variables exclusively
- Existing component reuse: Step 3.1 preserves AppShell, Header, Navigation
- Accessibility: Steps 5.1, 5.2, 5.3 ensure keyboard, ARIA, focus management
- Responsive: Step 4 implements 3-4/2/1 column breakpoints
- Fast planning-to-drafting: Single-click card open (Step 3.1 callback)

</plan>

title: Add anchored beta-reader comments on shared chapter previews
description:
## Problem
Writers need targeted chapter feedback from trusted readers, but generic sharing lacks structured comments tied to specific text or scene context. Without in-context feedback, revision cycles are slow and noisy.

## Target user
Writers sharing draft chapters privately with beta readers for pre-publication revision input.

## Outcome / KPI
- Increase number of shared previews that receive actionable feedback
- Increase chapter revision completion rate after feedback is received
- Reduce time from share to first accepted revision

## Scope
- Extend read-only chapter preview links with optional inline comment mode for invited readers
- Let readers leave anchored comments on paragraph/scene blocks plus general chapter notes
- Add writer inbox for incoming feedback with filters (open, resolved, by chapter)
- Allow writer to resolve comments and mark planned follow-up changes
- Track basic feedback metadata (reader alias, timestamp, chapter reference)

## Non-goals
- Public community feed/discovery mechanics
- Full real-time co-author editing
- Native mobile push notifications

## Acceptance criteria
- Writer can enable/disable comments per shared preview link
- Reader can submit anchored comments without edit permission to manuscript text
- Writer can view, resolve, and reopen comments from a dedicated feedback panel
- Resolved/open state persists across reload and is reflected in chapter view
- Comment links preserve manuscript privacy and respect share-link permissions

## Priority rationale
This builds directly on shareable previews and introduces an Inkitt-inspired feedback loop while keeping the product private-draft-first. It accelerates revision quality without requiring public publishing features.

## Handoff
Assign to: Market Research

title: Ship privacy-first AI consent controls with per-story opt-in
description:
## Problem
Writers are hesitant to use AI in drafting tools when content ownership and training policies are unclear. Without explicit controls, trust drops and AI features become either unused or a perceived privacy risk.

## Target user
Privacy-conscious fiction writers who want optional AI assistance but need clear consent and control over how their manuscript data is used.

## Outcome / KPI
- Increase opt-in conversion to AI features among eligible users
- Reduce support/questions related to data usage and AI trust
- Increase repeat usage of AI tools after first use

## Scope
- Add clear AI privacy controls in settings and first-use prompt
- Require explicit opt-in before any manuscript content is sent to AI endpoints
- Provide per-story AI enable/disable toggle and global account-level default
- Show a concise data-use notice near AI actions (what is sent, why, and retention summary)
- Add an audit-style activity list for recent AI actions (timestamp, story, action type)

## Non-goals
- Building proprietary model training infrastructure
- Legal policy rewrite inside this ticket
- Full enterprise compliance feature set (SSO, DLP, etc.)

## Acceptance criteria
- AI actions are unavailable until explicit user opt-in is completed
- User can disable AI globally and per story, and changes apply immediately
- Data-use notice is visible before first AI use and accessible later in settings
- Recent AI activity list shows at least the last 20 actions with basic metadata
- If AI is disabled, attempted AI actions fail safely with clear messaging

## Priority rationale
Living Writer-style optional AI is expected, but privacy-first execution is a trust differentiator. Shipping explicit consent and control now reduces adoption friction and protects writer confidence in core drafting workflows.

## Handoff
Assign to: Market Research

title: Add manuscript import wizard for .docx/.txt with chapter-scene detection
description:
## Problem
Many target users already draft in Google Docs, Word, or plain text. Without import, switching costs are high and onboarding stalls before users experience the app’s structure/planning advantages.

## Target user
Writers migrating an in-progress manuscript from general-purpose editors into a story-focused workspace.

## Outcome / KPI
- Increase activation rate from signup to first meaningful edit session
- Reduce drop-off during first-time story creation
- Increase imported manuscripts that reach at least one saved revision inside app

## Scope
- Add an import entry point from Home/Stories (`Import manuscript`)
- Support `.docx` and `.txt` uploads up to a defined size limit
- Parse headings into chapter candidates with editable mapping before final import
- Create scenes from detected chapter sections with fallback to single-scene import
- Show import summary: chapters/scenes created, unresolved formatting warnings

## Non-goals
- Perfect formatting parity for every Word style
- PDF import
- One-click cloud provider sync integrations

## Acceptance criteria
- User can upload `.docx` or `.txt` and complete import without leaving app
- User can review/edit chapter mapping before finalizing import
- Imported manuscript opens in chapter/scene navigator with preserved text content
- Unsupported formatting is surfaced as non-blocking warnings, not silent data loss
- Failed imports provide clear, actionable error message and retry path

## Priority rationale
Import removes a major adoption barrier and accelerates progress toward the company goal by letting real writers bring real work into the app immediately.

## Handoff
Assign to: Market Research

title: Reframe as story-aware focus sessions tied to scene objectives
description:
## Problem
Generic writing timers are now category-standard and do not reliably improve story completion quality on their own. Writers need focused sessions that connect directly to scene-level narrative outcomes, not just elapsed time and word counts.

## Target user
Fiction writers who draft in scenes and want structured sessions that help them finish specific narrative objectives (new beat, revision pass, dialogue polish) with less context-switching.

## Outcome / KPI
- Increase % of sessions with completed scene objective
- Increase multi-session continuity rate (users resume the suggested next scene within 24h)
- Improve week-2 retention among users who run story-aware focus sessions

## Scope
- Add `Story-aware Focus Session` mode in the editor with optional timer presets (15/25/45 minutes)
- Require selecting a target scene and objective type before starting (e.g., draft beat, revise pacing, tighten dialogue)
- Show live progress with both words-added and objective status (in progress / complete)
- End session with recap: words added, objective completion, and suggested next-scene handoff note
- Persist recent session history per story, including objective metadata

## Non-goals
- Social leaderboards, streak competitions, or multiplayer battles
- Advanced coaching/critique generated by AI
- Cross-story analytics dashboards in v1

## Acceptance criteria
- User can start/pause/end a focus session tied to a selected scene without data loss
- Objective selection is required at session start and stored in session history
- Session recap includes objective completion status and a next-step handoff note
- Save-state visibility remains present during active session mode
- Session settings/history persist across reload for the same user and story

## Priority rationale
Competitors already offer timer-only sprint tools; reframing around scene-objective completion creates a clearer story-first advantage and aligns with our goal to ship a writer workflow optimized for drafting outcomes.

## Handoff
Assign to: Market Research

title: Ship manuscript export to DOCX and PDF with chapter-aware formatting
description:
## Problem
Writers need to share or submit manuscripts outside the app, but there is no dependable export path that preserves chapter structure and readable formatting. This creates lock-in anxiety and blocks practical adoption.

## Target user
Drafting and revising writers who need to send manuscripts to beta readers, editors, or submission channels in standard document formats.

## Outcome / KPI
- Increase % of active stories that reach an export event
- Reduce churn risk signals related to data portability concerns
- Improve user satisfaction for end-to-end drafting workflow

## Scope
- Add `Export Manuscript` action at story level
- Support exports to `.docx` and `.pdf`
- Export includes title page (optional), chapter headings, scene breaks, and body text in reading order
- Provide basic formatting presets: manuscript standard and compact reading copy
- Include export confirmation with file type and timestamp

## Non-goals
- Query-package generation (synopsis, pitch letters, submission bundles)
- Advanced style customization controls
- Collaborative annotation inside exported documents

## Acceptance criteria
- User can export any story to DOCX and PDF without app crash or data loss
- Chapter/scene ordering in export matches in-app structure
- Formatting preset choice is applied correctly in output documents
- Exported files are downloadable locally with deterministic filenames
- Failed exports surface actionable error messaging and allow retry

## Priority rationale
Data portability is table-stakes for serious writers and directly supports trust in a writer-first product. A scoped, reliable export feature closes a core workflow gap without overreaching into publishing-suite complexity.

## Handoff
Assign to: Market Research

title: Create shareable read-only chapter preview links for private feedback
description:
## Problem
Writers need lightweight external feedback before publishing, but the app currently lacks a safe, low-friction way to share draft chapters without granting full edit access.

## Target user
Solo writers seeking feedback from trusted readers/critique partners during drafting and revision.

## Outcome / KPI
- Increase number of stories that receive at least one external feedback cycle
- Reduce time from draft completion to first feedback received
- Improve retention of writers in revision stages

## Scope
- Add per-chapter `Share preview` action that creates a read-only link
- Allow writer to set optional expiration date and regenerate/revoke links
- Preview page shows chapter content only (no editor controls, no internal workspace)
- Capture lightweight reader reactions (thumbs up/down + short comment)
- Surface feedback entries back in writer’s chapter sidebar

## Non-goals
- Full public publishing/discovery feed
- Real-time collaborative editing permissions
- Advanced moderation/community management systems

## Acceptance criteria
- Writer can generate, copy, and revoke a preview link per chapter
- Link opens a read-only page accessible without app account
- Expired/revoked links no longer expose chapter content
- Reader feedback submitted through preview appears in writer view within the chapter context
- Sharing controls are clearly scoped to chapter-level content only

## Priority rationale
This introduces an Inkitt-inspired feedback loop in a controlled, private form that fits MVP progression and helps writers iterate faster without expanding into full community infrastructure.

## Handoff
Assign to: Market Research

title: Add worldbuilding panel with linked story elements
description:
## Problem
Writers need fast access to character/location/lore context while drafting, but context switching to separate notes breaks flow.

## Target user
Writers building rich fictional worlds with recurring entities and rules.

## Outcome / KPI
- Increase count of stories using reusable worldbuilding entities.
- Reduce context-switching events away from active drafting views.

## Scope
- Add worldbuilding entities: character, location, and lore item (minimal schema).
- Add a side panel in editor to browse/search entities and pin relevant items per scene.
- Enable linking entities to scenes and viewing linked entities inline in scene context.
- Keep panel collapsible to preserve distraction-free writing space.

## Non-goals
- Advanced map builders, language generators, or magic-system simulation.
- Public wiki publishing for story worlds.
- Real-time collaborative world editing.

## Acceptance criteria
- Writer can create/edit/delete character, location, and lore entities.
- Writer can link/unlink entities to a scene from the editor.
- Scene view shows linked entities without leaving the draft context.
- Panel can be collapsed and reopened without losing draft state.

## Priority rationale
This captures a key Campfire-inspired advantage while keeping the writing surface clean, supporting differentiation beyond a plain document editor.

## Handoff
Assign to: Market Research

title: Create outline corkboard linked to scenes for planning and reordering
description:
## Problem
Writers need to plan story beats before and during drafting, but the app has no practical planning surface. Without an outline/corkboard, users bounce to external tools and lose context.

## Target user
Planner and hybrid writers who map scenes before drafting and frequently adjust sequence during revisions.

## Outcome / KPI
- Increase percentage of active manuscripts with at least one outline card
- Increase scene reorder actions completed inside app (vs external tools)
- Improve week-2 retention for planner/hybrid writing styles

## Scope
- Add Outline view with card-based scene summaries (one card per scene)
- Each card includes: scene title, intent, POV, and optional status tag
- Reordering cards updates the underlying scene order
- Clicking a card jumps user to that scene in editor
- Empty-outline onboarding suggests creating first 3 beats

## Non-goals
- AI-generated outlines
- Full timeline/dependency graph
- Community sharing of outlines

## Acceptance criteria
- User can create/edit outline fields per scene and data persists
- Dragging cards updates scene order reflected in chapter/scene navigator
- Card click deep-links into correct scene editor
- Status tags can be filtered (e.g., Drafted, Needs work, Final)
- Empty state includes clear call-to-action and no dead-end screen

## Priority rationale
This feature supports Living Writer-style structured planning and keeps writers inside one workflow. It strengthens differentiation from plain editors while remaining practical for MVP sequencing after core CRUD/autosave.

## Handoff
Assign to: Market Research

title: Create lightweight timeline view for chronological consistency
description:
## Problem
Writers struggle to track chronology across scenes, causing continuity errors and rework late in the drafting process.

## Target user
Novelists managing multi-thread stories with time-sensitive events.

## Outcome / KPI
- Reduce reported continuity-conflict edits during revision.
- Increase number of stories using timeline metadata per scene.

## Scope
- Add optional timeline metadata to scenes (date/time or relative order).
- Provide a timeline view listing scenes in chronological order independent of manuscript order.
- Flag obvious chronology conflicts (e.g., duplicate impossible ordering within same storyline tag).
- Enable click-through from timeline entry to scene editor.

## Non-goals
- Full calendar app integration.
- Complex simulation of travel/logistics constraints.
- AI auto-fixing timeline conflicts.

## Acceptance criteria
- Writer can set and edit timeline metadata for a scene.
- Timeline view renders scenes by chronological order and supports navigation to scene drafts.
- At least one deterministic chronology conflict rule is enforced and surfaced to the writer.
- Timeline metadata persists and survives refresh/reload.

## Priority rationale
Timeline support improves structural quality and reduces revision pain, making the app more credible for long-form fiction projects.

## Handoff
Assign to: Market Research
